method3.generated_article:
==Name==
The name Brecqhou refers specifically to the smaller island west of Sark, while the name Sark may refer either to the neighbouring island alone or to the wider jurisdiction that includes Sark, Brecqhou and nearby islets and rocks.[1] The island is also recorded with the alternative spelling Brechou in descriptions of Sark and its geography.[5] Historically, after ownership passed through the Le Marchant family, Brecqhou became known as Isle des Marchants, and that name continued to be used even after ownership later passed to others.[2] The modern name is also preserved in Fort Brecqhou, the mock-Gothic residence built on the island by the Barclay brothers.[4]

==Geography==
Brecqhou is one of the inhabited Channel Islands and forms part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, a group of islands in the English Channel off the coast of Normandy.[2][3] It lies adjacent to Great Sark, from which it is separated by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[4] In regional terms, Sark lies about 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France, placing Brecqhou within the same central Channel Islands setting.[4] Sark and Brecqhou together are given an area of 2.1 square miles (5.5 km²).[4] The island is privately owned and has a very small population compared with Sark, a physical and demographic separation that has been noted in discussions of the Sark–Brecqhou island relationship.[1] Geologically, Brecqhou belongs to the older rock assemblages of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, where metamorphic rocks such as schists and gneisses occur on most of Sark and Brecqhou.[5] These rocks record deformation and metamorphism dating to early Proterozoic time, around 2,020 million years ago.[5]

==Flag and stamps==
Brecqhou has occasionally appeared in informal online material about flags, but the available source does not establish a formally recognised island flag or give authoritative symbolism for one.[1] Academic writing on Brecqhou has instead concentrated on its contested relationship with Sark, private ownership and jurisdictional status, rather than on separate civic symbols.[2][4]  

Philatelic matters connected with Brecqhou fall within the broader postal history of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, whose postal administration developed from informal carriage arrangements to a formal post office in 1794 and postal independence in 1969.[5] Guernsey’s status has enabled distinctive local stamp issues while retaining historical links to the British postal system, providing the wider postal context for Sark and associated islands.[5] The cited postal-history account describes Guernsey’s stamp-issuing framework and does not identify a separate official Brecqhou postal administration.[5]

==Public visits==
Public access to Brecqhou is restricted, as visitation to the privately owned island is by invitation only.[4] The island lies off the west coast of Sark, about 0.5 km from Sark, and is within Sark’s jurisdiction in the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[1][4][5] Brecqhou was sold to the Barclay brothers in 1993, after which access remained controlled rather than generally open to visitors.[4] This contrasts with some other Channel Islands, such as Herm, which is described as open to the public.[1]

==Notes and references==
Academic and official sources often treat Brecqhou in connection with Sark, and one study notes that references to Sark as a political jurisdiction may implicitly include Brecqhou unless the two islands are distinguished separately.[2]

A useful caution in interpreting sources is that Brecqhou has sometimes been discussed as physically and culturally separate from Sark, particularly in relation to private owners’ use of emblems such as flags and carriage-label stamp issues.[2]

Modern scholarly discussion of Brecqhou frequently centres on its contested relationship with Sark, including disputes involving Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and debates over Sark’s institutions, development and governance.[3]

Cartographic sources for the Bailiwick include locally produced mapping rather than Ordnance Survey coverage, with the States of Guernsey map including Sark, Jethou and Brecqhou among the mapped islands.[4]

Brecqhou is also mentioned in wider Channel Islands scholarship, including linguistic work that lists it among the islands while focusing on the Norman Romance varieties historically spoken across the archipelago.[1]

Natural-history and travel accounts commonly mention Brecqhou from viewpoints or boat routes around Sark, including observations of its coastline and the mansion associated with the Barclay brothers.[5]

==Fort Brecqhou==
Fort Brecqhou was designed by British architect John Quinlan Terry for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and was built between 1994 and 1996, shortly after the brothers purchased the Brecqhou tenement in 1993 for £2.3 million.[2] It has been described as the largest house built in Britain for at least two centuries, with the house, gardens and outbuildings estimated to have cost about £90 million.[1][2] The building is a granite-clad, castellated Gothic country house laid out on a quadrangular plan, with circular turrets at its four corners.[2] Its architectural detailing includes large twisted chimneys in a style associated with the reign of Henry VIII, which the DiCamillo Companion notes as a distinctively English architectural form.[2] The structure was built using two types of Spanish granite and has been characterised in architectural commentary as a deliberately fortress-like expression of permanence and privacy.[1] The property remains a private residence, and public access is limited to the grounds by appointment.[2]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
Sark’s feudal framework was formalised in 1565, when Elizabeth I granted Helier de Carteret the Seignory as a fief haubert, requiring personal homage to the monarch and a form of knight service.[2] In return, de Carteret received extensive rights over the land and surrounding sea, together with broad legal and political authority, while being charged with keeping the island free from the Queen’s enemies.[2] One interpretation links Brecqhou to that defensive obligation: because of its proximity to Sark, de Carteret’s duty to protect the fief with armed men could be read as including Brecqhou, placing it within Sark’s fief alongside associated islets, reefs and rocks.[1] This interpretation has been disputed, however, with Rivett arguing that Brecqhou was not originally part of the fief, particularly because it was not a tenement and was only later claimed.[1]

Brecqhou’s status has therefore been described as historically contested, especially in relation to whether it should be regarded as a tenement of Sark.[4] Rivett asserted that Dame Sibyl Hathaway had unlawfully bestowed on Brecqhou the status of a Sark tenement and a seat in Chief Pleas, while Dawes has also been cited as suggesting that Brecqhou is not a “tenement of Sark”.[4] Later conveyancing material nevertheless treated Brecqhou as a dependency of Sark, with 1949 legal advice stating that Sark law applied and that Brecqhou’s tenure was similar to that of other Sark tenements.[4] Modern official descriptions have generally accepted Brecqhou as falling within Sark’s jurisdiction, including statements that the Bailiwick of Guernsey comprises separate jurisdictions including Sark, “which includes the island of Brecqhou”.[1]

Because Brecqhou has been treated as a tenement within the fief, Sark’s legislative body, Chief Pleas, has applied Sark laws to it.[1] Some Sark laws have nevertheless been disapplied to Brecqhou, giving it a special status and a limited degree of autonomy compared with other tenements.[1] The island’s private owners have been central to modern disputes over Sark’s feudal institutions, most notably after Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay purchased Brecqhou and challenged seigneurial privileges.[3] Their objections included liability for the treizième, a feudal payment of one-thirteenth of a property purchase price to the Seigneur, which was abolished in 2007 and replaced by a property transfer tax payable to the island.[3] Sark’s feudal government was replaced in 2008 by an elected assembly, following wider pressure for democratic reform in which the Barclay brothers and their Sark News campaign played a prominent role.[1][5]
